description: Trigger discrimination for the highest-risk seam in the data pack: INTERNAL streaming backbone (this skill) vs EXTERNAL API/event contracts (api-event-architect), the worker EXECUTION model that rides it (background-job-orchestration-architect — the DLQ/retry seam), live CLIENT push (realtime-subscription-architect), plus the audit trail (audit-log-architect), the split decision (operational-vs-analytical-splitter), and database schema evolution (schema-evolution-planner).

cases.prompt: Design how our billing, inventory, and notification services exchange events internally — topics, partition keys, what happens on consumer failure, and how we replay after an outage.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary) — every consumer named is an internal service. Topics, keys, DLQ, and replay are pipeline mechanics, not a contract offered to outsiders. No signing, no subscriptions, no partner versioning appears anywhere in the ask.
cases.reason: Internal service-to-service event flow with failure and replay design is the backbone's core scope. api-event-architect owns none of these mechanics — it owns the promise made to external integrators.

cases.prompt: Expose our domain events to customers and partners: versioned event envelope, tenant-scoped subscriptions, webhook signing and replay protection, and a deprecation policy for old event versions.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary, reverse direction) — every requirement is contract surface: envelope versioning for OUTSIDERS, subscription scoping, signing, sunset policy. The internal stream that feeds this surface is upstream plumbing and is not what was asked for.
cases.reason: Signed, versioned, tenant-scoped delivery to external consumers is the public contract. The streaming skill explicitly stops at the boundary where internal streams hand events to that layer.

cases.prompt: We need a durable record of every admin action and data export — actor, tenant, outcome — tamper-evident, with retention rules. It'll probably ride our event stream.
cases.reason: Actor/action/outcome records with tamper-evidence and retention are the compliance audit trail — audit-log-architect owns the taxonomy, schema, and integrity guarantees even when the transport happens to be a stream. Transport choice does not transfer ownership.

cases.prompt: Our dashboards and exports are hammering the production database. Should that reporting workload move off it, and which parts?
cases.reason: WHETHER and WHICH workloads leave the operational store is the splitter's decision. CDC-into-streams may be its prescription — and only then does the streaming skill design that transport.

cases.prompt: We're renaming a column on a hot table and several services read it directly — sequence the change so deploys don't break.
cases.reason: The evolving artifact is a database object with overlapping deployed readers — the expand/migrate/contract sequence. Event-schema compatibility rules only apply when the payload is a stream event, which this is not.

cases.prompt: Design how our background workers run jobs: idempotency and retry-with-backoff per job, visibility timeouts, a dead-letter queue with an owner, resumable long jobs, and fairness so one tenant can't monopolize the workers.
cases.note: SEAM CASE — the DLQ/retry seam: transport-level dead-lettering (delivery attempts, retention) is this skill; a job that keeps failing its handler with a retry policy and an owner is the worker execution model.
cases.reason: Per-job retry/backoff, visibility timeouts, DLQ ownership, checkpointing, and worker fairness are the EXECUTION model (background-job-orchestration-architect). This skill designs the TRANSPORT those jobs ride — topics, partitions, delivery semantics — not the worker on top of it.

cases.prompt: Clients should get live updates over WebSocket when a shared resource changes — subscribe-time authorization, cross-node fan-out, and reconnect that replays what they missed.
cases.reason: Live delivery to client connections (subscribe-time authz, fan-out, reconnect/replay) is realtime-subscription-architect. This skill designs the internal server-to-server backbone it may consume, not the push to client connections.
